topics.title: Early Radio Career and Technical Challenges
topics.summary: The studio recounts early experiences in radio broadcasting, including a stint at WNTN where pay was withheld, the complexities of manual meter readings for the FCC, and frustrations with television lighting during Howard TV appearances.

topics.title: Studio Production and Programming Logistics
topics.summary: The studio discusses technical camera placements, lighting issues from a previous episode, upcoming guest lineups including a roast and special features, and the selection of classic radio clips.

topics.title: Repetitive Audio Loop
topics.summary: The show features a continuous loop of the phrase "That's funny to play for now" repeated at every timestamp.

topics.title: Appearance and Media Appearances
topics.summary: The studio discusses the host's appearance on other networks, his use of dark glasses to hide his nose, and various personal anecdotes regarding relationships and television segments.

topics.title: Caller Interaction Regarding Employment and Career Advice
topics.summary: The studio hosts engage in a lengthy, confrontational conversation with a caller named Shavon regarding his lack of professional skills, physical limitations, and requests for career assistance.

topics.title: Career Advice and Radio Promotions
topics.summary: The studio discusses the challenges of career transitions for guests, the logistics of radio website management, and upcoming promotional giveaways.

topics.title: Madonna's Controversial Adoption
topics.summary: The studio discusses the legal and ethical complications surrounding Madonna's adoption of an African child whose biological father claims he did not intend to surrender his parental rights.

topics.title: Celebrity Gossip and Upcoming Guest Appearances
topics.summary: The studio discusses various celebrity news stories involving Madonna, Paul McCartney, and the Hogan family before preparing for an upcoming interview with Brooke Hogan.

topics.title: Hogan Family Dynamics and Marriage Stress
topics.summary: The studio explores the personal lives of Hulk Hogan and his wife Linda, discussing their marriage, the pressures of reality television, and the upbringing of their daughter Brooke.

topics.title: Musical Performance and Personal Anecdotes
topics.summary: The studio hosts a discussion featuring personal stories about family life, career milestones, and upcoming musical performances.

topics.title: Brooke Hogan Album Release and Performance
topics.summary: The show features Brooke Hogan discussing her upcoming album, performing a song with Paul, and promoting her appearance at the FYE in Manhattan.

topics.title: Chess Matches and Celebrity News
topics.summary: The studio features a discussion regarding an upcoming chess match between hosts, the news of Stephen Hawking's divorce, and various segments involving celebrity clips and sports betting.

topics.title: Radio Show Programming and News Segments
topics.summary: The studio discusses the merits of different show formats, reports on local news stories including a tragic misunderstanding involving a toddler's claims, and shares anecdotes regarding personal experiences with drugs.

topics.title: Current Events and Celebrity News
topics.summary: The show covers a variety of topics including the auction of Nazi memorabilia, advancements in invisibility technology, celebrity legal issues, and recent news regarding Steve Irwin's daughter.

topics.title: Current Events and Entertainment News
topics.summary: The show covers a variety of topics including a medical emergency involving a fish hook, the discovery of more 9/11 remains, the escalating situation in Iraq, potential presidential candidates, legal drama surrounding Paul McCartney, and recent box office highlights.

What was it, '76, you started on a station?
Yeah, I guess you got started. You don't count college radio, that's kind of gay.
Right.
Why not?
Although, when I was in college, I didn't get a job, but the guy wouldn't pay me.
I mean, he gave me a salary, but he just wouldn't hand it to me.
Oh, he wouldn't give you the money.
Does that count as a job?
Really?
He promised you money, but he didn't pay you.
Yeah, when you were here--
What was his explanation for that?
When you were just awful because people take advantage of you, there was this young guy who became the program director of a progressive radio station.
Progressive means you can play whatever kind of music you want, WNTN in Newton, Massachusetts, AM station.
Day timer means it's only on from 6 a.m. till sunrise to sunset.
Right.
And I got a job there, which was unbelievable. This was a legendary radio station.
Can you imagine an AM radio station progressive?
And I get a job while I'm in college beside myself.
You never told us you had this kind of luck?
Unbelievable luck.
I went down there and they hired me to do an air shift.
A progressive to me sounds like a hippie type station.
Yeah, but one of the coolest stations in the country, legendary.
It was like, you know, in the Boston area, there was WBCN and WNTN was its predecessor.
It was like a big deal to work there.
And I got hired to be a dish jockey.
Cool.
And this station was on its last legs. It was a dinosaur.
Day timers were on their way out, yeah.
In fact, into an M.O.R. station, which is middle of the road, kind of like the, you know, they played everything from Perry Como to, you know, just like wimpy shit.
They're actually...
Dean Martin and Sammy Davis Jr. probably.
Yeah, and also...
There's actually an acronym in the business, they call that M.O.R.
Middle of the road. I don't think they called that anymore, but back then it was M.O.R.
Adult contempt or something.
Right. So now it's M.O.R.
But I didn't know that.
I get hired and, wow, I mean, I was awful, you know, I was just the worst dish jockey.
I don't even know why they hired me, I guess. Now I know why, because...
So the guy hired me and my salary was, you know, a couple of bucks an hour, whatever it was.
But this was, I called my father, oh my God, I'm in the radio business.
I got a job!
And in fact, I had no way to even get there to Newton, Massachusetts.
So he helped me with a car, a whole thing, you know.
Oh, gee, so he went into his pocket for this job.
He did, he did, he did. But he knew it was a big deal.
And...
Your father knew it was a big deal.
Yeah, oh, he understood.
I mean, for me to have a job while I was in college at a real radio station...
But that's cool that he understood that, he got that.
No, he helped me out, he only helped me out.
Well, he was in broadcasting, he never expected, I guess, Howard would ever get a job.
No, I certainly didn't.
And not out of school yet.
So I get hired by the guy, I can still picture his face, I don't remember his name.
Young guy, former BU student, actually.
And he liked me for some reason, now I know why.
So I go there and I'm doing the job as best I can.
And I'm working there, and it comes payday.
No pay!
So now I feel like an asshole, asking the guy...
You mean, there was a day you were supposed to get paid?
Yeah, you know, a general area.
Did you see other people getting paid?
I didn't know any other people, I did the show and the other person would take...
I was just so thrilled that I had a job, I mean, I had a real job.
And I was awful, I couldn't even understand why...
And you were showing up every day for work and everything?
Yeah, and the other jocks were so good.
What was the paycheck for, do you remember?
Yeah, how much was it paid?
It was a couple of dollars an hour, it might have been, like in the four or five dollar an hour range, or six dollars maybe.
So you were going to check about 80 bucks or something?
Something, but to be paid would have been my first professional job.
And actually, forget the pay, at least I'd have tape from WNTN that I could play it on the radio.
There's a million positives about it, absolutely.
But I was so awful, I couldn't figure out why they hired me, and then I understood.
So days go by and you get paid, and I went to them and I said, "I didn't get a paycheck."
Oh, and I think I know what you're going to say.
And the guy said to me, "Listen, you're lucky you're working here."
Working, it's not working if you're not getting paid.
Did he give you, you're in college rap? Like you're almost an intern?
He goes, "Yeah." He goes, "Listen, you're horrible on the radio."
And this is great experience for you.
If you want to leave, go fucking leave.
Fuck you. That's what he said.
But he offered you pay.
What a douchebag.
That's how he got you there.
Go argue with the guy, find out his name. You can pick it up with him.
What a rotten thing to do.
We should find out his name.
I got to look at him. I want to know how I would find his name.
It might have been 1975 or something like that.
That is funny.
Yeah, so I worked at WNT in Newton for a couple of weeks.
And I think I stayed there for a little while because his logic was correct.
He didn't even string out a little while and say, "Oh, it's coming.
You need to get on the roll or something."
Didn't even bother me.
No, he's just like, "Let's just fuck you and leave, and you're not doing anything.
Go work at your free college radio station rather than be heard."
And he was right.
That's a rotten guy.
So the great thing about this guy is he was so in over his head because he wasn't probably getting paid.
He said, "I'm not getting paid either."
So he did the public affairs.
That makes sense, I guess.
Back then, you had to buy FCC law.
You had to have a public affairs show on Sunday, which he was in charge of.
I guess he taped his show.
Beforehand, he was like, "This fucking equipment doesn't work.
I can't fucking get this thing fucking right."
So they had this Sunday morning tape, and it's him going, "I can't get this fucking thing right."
He left all that off.

The FCC, I said fuck 40 times.
I said, "Well, maybe they won't hear it."
He goes, "No, I have to turn myself in."
So this fucking idiot, this guy was such an idiot.
He called the FCC.
The idiot, I was there.
He called the FCC and reported himself.
They threw himself on the mercy of the FCC, and they didn't even know what he was talking about.
They probably don't even know that thing's just on the air.
No one knew that.
You guys are on the air?
They didn't know what he was talking about.
He wrote them a letter.
Who did what?
He documented on himself that he said fuck.
He had to turn himself in.
Who told him that?

Yeah, but I never heard the rule that you had to turn yourself in.
They'd go easy on you.

every minute they would tell you, "Listen, there's probably an F--"
You had to take meter readings in the early days of radio because--
not the early days of radio, but my career--
they didn't have an engineer or anything.

And I didn't know how to take these meter readings.
No matter how many times they explained it to me.
It was a metric, and it was horrible.
It was little dials and gauges.
You had to be a scientist.
And I used to make up my meter readings, always made up my meter readings.
I just-- whatever the guy wrote down before, I'd write down the same thing and this and that.
Vary it by a degree or something.
Yeah, what the fuck did I care?
So then they would say to you, "Listen, you better take your meter readings today.
The FCC field marshal is in town, and he's going to be listening."
Field marshal.

and if they don't match exactly what you write down,
it's a big violation of the end of your career.
Now, the reason they told you that is so you would take taking your meter readings seriously.
There was no FCC.
Yeah, they always told you, "Oh, there's somebody out there listening."
Yeah.
"They travel around in a car."
Yeah, it was always a just imaginary FCC guy.
And I was like, "Oh, shit."
Oh, shit.
And not only that, it wasn't just the meter readings.
It was the log.
Remember you had to log in.
The log, yeah.
Well, that's how you wrote it down.
Your commercials and your ID and all that stuff.
Yeah, and like I didn't even know how to do that.
Like, I had to keep a log.
Like, if I played a commercial, and if I played it right now, it would be six.

Yeah.

Oh, God.

And you're doing a radio show, and you're writing all this down.
And I used to have nightmares about doing a radio show.
I couldn't get all this done.

so I had to write in the numbers the right way.

Why should we tell him?
